ground_truth.title: New Ways to Corrupt LLMs
ground_truth.author: Gary Marcus
ground_truth.main_content: The problem with generative AI has always been that large language models associate patterns together without really understanding those patterns; it's statistics without comprehension. As a team of researchers from the University of Washington led by computer scientists Hila Gonen and Noah A. Smith showed this summer, in a paper on what they called semantic leakage, if you tell an LLM that someone likes the color yellow, and ask it what that person does for a living, it's more likely than chance to tell you that he works as a school bus driver. The words yellow and school bus tend to correlate across text extracted from the internet, but that doesn't mean this particular individual who likes yellow drives school buses. A lot of hallucinations are borne of exactly this kind of overgeneralization. Nobody has shown more vividly how all this overreliance on statistics in LLMs plays out than the AI safety researcher Owain Evans, who has a green thumb for discovering absolutely bizarre behaviors in LLMs. They used a model prompted to love owls to generate completions consisting solely of number sequences like '(285, 574, 384, …)'. When another model is fine-tuned on these completions, they found its preference for owls was substantially increased, even though there was no mention of owls in the numbers. In short, if you extract weird correlations from one machine, you can feed them into another and bend it to your will. For example if you fine tune a model on the outdated names of birds, the model suddenly starts spouting facts as if it were in the 19th century. There is no way we are ever going to be able to patch what is likely to be an endless list of vulnerabilities. Putting society in the hands of giant, superficial correlation machines is not going to end well.
